comments.text: A brute force computation of $f(n)$ for all $n\leq e^{30.1}$ is not enough to give us a clear indication if $\frac{f(n)}{\log n}$ is bounded or not. The following figure summarizes the main results of the entire computation. For each integer belonging to the interval $[e^{5+0.2k-0.1},e^{5+0.2k+0.1}[$, with $k$ a non-negative integer (for $k=0$ the lower interval limit was replaced by $1$), $f(n)$ was evaluated and the number of occurrences of each $f(n)$ value was recorded. Prime values of $f(n)$ are somewhat more frequent that non-prime values. TOS P.S. to the moderators (please remove this comment after dealing with what follows). Would it be better to embed directly the PDF figure in my comment? Assuming, of course, that the comment is accepted. Also, for long term stability, it will be better if the PDF is hosted directly in erdosproblems.com. Assume a creative commons licence for it.
comments.meta: Tomas Oliveira e Silva — 13:33 on 20 May 2026 👍 1 📝 0 🤖 0

comments.text: Unbounded logarithmic limsup in Erdős problem 684 https://zenodo.org/records/19807164

The OpenAI proof seems to have been optimized for brevity rather than explainability, and in general reads like "here is the first working proof that we found" rather than "here is a streamlined proof highlighting the key ideas", but it follows broadly similar lines to previous (successful or unsuccessful) attempts to upper bound $f(n)$ in the comments here. In all cases, the first step is to use Kummer's theorem to observe that a prime $p$ will divide $u$ whenever $n \% p < k \% p$, where I use $n \% p = p \{\frac{n}{p} \}$ to denote the remainder of $n$ after dividing by $p$. (Kummer's theorem also gives contributions when $n \% p^r < k \% p^r$ for higher prime powers $p^r$, but these contributions are lower order and so not of much additional use.) So to upper bound $f(n)$, one has to find a $k$ that is not too large such that $n \% p < k \% p$ for a lot of primes $p$. One should presumably focus on primes $p$ that are not much larger than $k$, otherwise the right-hand side $k \% p$ is small and the condition is unlikely to be satisfied. So one should think of $k$ and $p$ as both being small. We get to pick the $k$ and $p$ that we want, so we can take advantage of the probabilistic method and average (or take expectations) in these parameters with regard to suitably chosen probability distributions. The main enemy comes from the fact that $n \% p$ could be very close to the maximal value of $p$, in which case the condition is unlikely to be satisfied. If one had some good equidistribution theory on $\{ n/p\}$ then this would be easy to avoid, and this is basically why one can get bounds on $f(n)$ of polynomial type (actually using Vinogradov exponential sum estimates one could probably get a bound of the shape $f(n) \ll \exp(\log^{2/3+o(1)} n)$ from an equidistribution approach). But when $k$ or $p$ is only polylogarithmic in size then such equidistribution results are not known currently (the difficulty level here is comparable to the Riemann hypothesis). The new idea of the OpenAI proof is to use an elementary upper bound on the rare event that $n \% p$ is close to $p$ that is weaker than equidistribution, but still gives something useful. Specifically, if $n\% p \geq p-A$ for some $A$, then $p$ divides $(n+1) \dots (n+A)$. In particular, if $A$ and $Y$ are of polylogarithmic size, then the number of primes $p \asymp Y$ for which $n \% p \geq p-A$ is at most $O( A \log n / \log Y)$. Comparing this with the prime number theorem, we see that for a given scale $Y$, about $\gg Y / \log Y$ of the primes $p \asymp Y$ will have $n \% p < p - A$ for some $A \asymp Y / \log n$. (Equidistribution predicts that this $\log n$ denominator can be eliminated; this loss of $\log n$ is ultimately why the OpenAI paper gets a $\log^2 n$ type bound on $f$ rather than the conjectural $\log^{1+o(1)} n$ bound.) Anyway, for a prime $p$ of the above form, the condition $n \% p < k \% p$ would now hold with probability $\gg A / p \asymp 1 / \log n$ for $k$ drawn randomly in the range $k = O(Y)$. Summing over such primes, we conclude that the expected value of $\log u$ is $\gg \frac{Y}{\log Y} \frac{1}{\log n} \log Y$. Setting $Y = C \log^2 n$ for a large $C$, one can then ensure that $\log u > 2 \log n$ for some $k = O(Y)$, which gives the upper bound $f(n) \ll \log^2 n$. The actual proof is a bit more intricate (in particular introducing a new summation variable $j$ that is not present in the above discussion) because it is trying to optimize the explicit constant in this bound. One should be able to mostly eliminate this logarithmic loss if one is also permitted to average in $n$. In particular I expect these methods should be able to show that $f(n) \ll \log^{1+o(1)} n$ for almost all $n$, or something like this.

Thanks for the insights! Building on this, GPT-5.4 Thinking is able to prove that, for almost all $n$: $$ f(n) \le \left( \frac{4}{1-\gamma} + o(1) \right) \log n = (9.461\ldots + o(1)) \log n, $$ where $\gamma$ is the Euler-Mascheroni constant. Here are the notes.

Since many people are now using AI to explore problems on this site, I tried this problem using ChatGPT-5.2 Thinking and obtained a nontrivial upper bound:\[f(n)\le \Bigl\lceil n^{\frac{12}{17}+\varepsilon}\Bigr\rceil \qquad (\forall\,\varepsilon>0,\ n\ge n_0(\varepsilon)).\]A full write-up is in this note. Verification. I audited the final lemma--proof write-up in multiple fresh and independent sessions of ChatGPT-5.2 Thinking. All audits agreed that the proof is logically correct, up to minor presentational clarifications. EDIT: I have also carefully reviewed the proof myself and found no issues with it. How the result was obtained. The entire derivation was produced by the AI model, without human intervention in the mathematical reasoning or idea generation. I repeatedly asked ChatGPT-5.2 Thinking for stronger unconditional upper bounds for $f(n)$: it started from a basic bound $f(n)\le n/2$ and was iteratively strengthened, eventually yielding the polynomial improvement above. The full prompt history is recorded in the appendix of this note. P.S. This is my first time trying to tackle a problem on this site using AI end-to-end, and I am not sure whether this kind of comment/approach is valuable to the community; I would be very happy to hear any feedback. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
It is a good idea to formalize proofs produced by AI to make sure they are actually valid. Try Aristotle!

Thanks! I haven't checked the details, but it looks like you get a better exponent of $30/43$ using the recent work of Guth and Maynard, and $2/3$ assuming RH (or the density hypothesis). Presumably the limit of this approach is $n^{1/2+o(1)}$, assuming the usual strong prime heuristics.

A preliminary check with ChatGPT confirms the note as well as what’s written in Bloom’s comment. About value: I think as per Tao I still prefer that a human goes through the argument in the final round to 1) refine writing, 2) make sure nothing feels off (as far as they can see). I suppose in the end we’re more confident if you yourself are more confident, so any method to make yourself more confident in the argument (e.g. targeted LLM conversation, manual rewriting) should work to increase value!

Thanks for the comments! I agree with Bloom's point. Following the same method in my note, one can plug in the recent work of Guth and Maynard [GuMa24] to obtain the stronger unconditional bound \(f(n) \le n^{30/43+o(1)}.\) Also, for clarity: the proof in the note was generated by the AI model; I carefully checked the argument myself, and only manually polished the introduction and some presentational details/technical steps in the proof. I did not share the raw chat logs because I used multiple independent chat sessions to audit each iteration of the argument, and most of those conversations were in Chinese (rather than English). Reference. [GuMa24] Guth, Larry, and James Maynard. "New large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials." arXiv preprint arXiv:2405.20552 (2024). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks for manual check! You can actually share the raw chat (the main one that derives result). There are a few reasons: 1) some people may want to know what the LLM says compared to what’s in the note, 2) there may be extra details there that people who work on the problem further may want to look through, 3) Chinese can easily be translated by the LLM itself.

Okay. I am sharing the complete transcripts of three ChatGPT-5.2 Thinking conversations in which I obtained the upper bound for $f(n)$: Problem 684_v1, Problem 684_v2, Problem 684_v3. I used these three chats to cross-check the proof details against each other. Moreover, I have only now realized that in order to reach the bound \(f(n)\le n^{12/17+o(1)},\) I did in fact perform some human intervention that required a fair amount of luck. Here is a brief summary of what happened. In Problem 684_v1, the model obtained the bound $f(n)=o(n)$. In Problem 684_v3, after it produced the upper bound \(f(n) \le n\exp(-c_1(\log n)^{3/5}(\log\log n)^{-1/5}),\) I asked whether one could obtain an even stronger bound. It then ''thought'' for a long time but ended up outputting nothing. However, I carefully read through its internal reasoning and noticed what looked like a promising line of thought that could lead to \(f(n)\le n^{12/17+\varepsilon}.\) I therefore copied and pasted that portion into a file 684_v5.txt, uploaded it in Problem 684_v2, and told the model: "The txt file I am sending you contains a brainstorming attempt by another LLM on this problem. Please see whether that brainstorm can lead to meaningful progress on the upper bound for $f(n)$." After that, it produced an initial proof of \(f(n)\le n^{12/17+\varepsilon}.\)
Thanks for the detailed explanation! Based on this, I am classifying this as a Section 4 partial result on the wiki.

OK yes, this proof looks good to me - although as written that method is capped out at $n^{1/2}$ (since in analysis $v$ one is taking advantage of the fact that primes $>n^{1/2}$ can divide $\binom{n}{k}$ at most once). It would be nice to see what a heuristic version of this argument gives (e.g. assuming ridiculously strong estimates for primes in short intervals, as the Poisson random model gives), since this would give a better idea of the true order of growth of $f(n)$.
Quanyu & Bloom: numerical evidence and heuristic argument places $f(n)$ to be on order of $O(\log n)$. See the note here, joint with ChatGPT.

Yes, this looks sensible. To summarise here for convenience, the observation is that\[\log v(n,k)=\sum_{k<p\leq n}\log p\sum_{j\geq 1}1_{n\pmod{p^j}<k},\]and assuming the indicator function here to be binomial with probability $k/p^j$ gives the heuristic\[\log v(n,k)=k\sum_{k<p\leq n}\frac{\log p}{p-1}=k\log(n/k)+o(k).\]Since $\log\binom{n}{k}=k\log(n/k)+k+O(\log k)$ by Stirling, this means we expect $u>n^2$, i.e. $\log\binom{n}{k}-\log v>2\log n$, at $k\sim 2\log n$. This certainly makes sense as an 'average' observation, but I do worry that the worst case behaviour (which is the problem here) might behave a little different - one can imagine some conspiracy between $n$ and 'medium-range' primes which forces $v$ to be measurably larger than this suggests. There is also a suspicious spike in the numerical data you give at $n\approx 10^5$, much higher than the predicted $2\log n$. It might be that $f(n)\sim 2\log n$ for almost all $n$, but perhaps for infinitely many $n$ we have $f(n)\gg (\log n)^2$, say.
Further computation supports $f(n)$ is possibly $\Theta(\log n)$. I have updated the note, which has a new plot.

Some more updates. Numerics suggests perhaps $\liminf f(n)/\log n \approx 1$ and $\limsup f(n)/\log n \approx 8$. So I was curious what these values are. ChatGPT gives some heuristic based on random walk that the $\limsup$ might be the solution of $c_* D(2/c_* || 1/2) = 1$, which is $c_* \approx 7.88$. But it also said that such model gives $\liminf \ge 2$, so the model cannot explain the $\liminf$.

I believe that a significantly better bound than the $f(n) \le \lceil n^{\frac{12}{17} + \varepsilon} \rceil $, Q.Tang and GPT-5.2 seem to have obtained is possible, being $f(n) \le C \ln(n) \ln(\ln(n))$ While I have not completely finished to prove it, I do think that I arrived quite close, and hope that other might help finish this proof. See here the pdf version for the details : https://github.com/Axurios/Erdos/blob/main/erdos684_draft.pdf And below the final state of where I arrived To prove that $f(n) \leq C \ln n \ln\ln n$, I was just missing that : There exists $c > 0$ such that,\[ \omega_k(\textstyle\binom{n}{k}) \ge c\, \pi(k) \quad \text{for} \quad k \sim C \ln n \ln(\ln n) \]for $\omega_k(\binom{n}{k})$ the count of distinct prime divisors of $\binom{n}{k}$ under $k$ The only part missing to prove the above is:\[ \omega_k{\textstyle({\binom{n}{k}})} \underset{??}{=} \underset{p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \left\{ \frac{k}{p} \right\} \,\, +\, o(\pi(k)) = (1-\gamma) \,\pi(k) \,+\, o(\pi(k)) \]for $\omega_k(\binom{n}{k})$ the count of distinct prime divisors of $\binom{n}{k}$ under $k$, and $k\sim \ln(n)\ln(\ln(n))$ PS: edited for brevity
Will assess!

ChatGPT gave a preliminary assessment which is essentially the same as Bloom's, i.e. the Lemma 2.1 is not proved and what's "missing" is not expected to be true. Some exposition note: if a claim is not proven, it's better to label it as "Claim" or "Conjecture", rather than "Lemma".

I don't see any reason to expect\[ \sum_{p\leq k} 1_{p\mid \binom{n}{k}}=\sum_{p\leq k}\left\{\frac{k}{p}\right\}+o(\pi(k))\]is true, even heuristically. If you are going this route it seems easier to not reduce down to $\omega_k$ and look at $\sum_{p\leq k}\nu_p$ directly, since this is what Kummer's theorem addresses anyway.

While I have not finished it completely yet, I have made further progress, that I believe give reason to expect\[ \omega_k{\textstyle({\binom{n}{k}})} = \underset{p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \left\{ \frac{k}{p} \right\} \,\, +\, o(\pi(k)) \]for $\omega_k(\binom{n}{k})$ the count of distinct prime divisors of $\binom{n}{k}$ under $k$, and $k\sim \ln(n)\ln(\ln(n))$ Which would allow us to conclude, $f(n) \leq C \ln(n) \ln\ln(n)$. Here is the pdf version for details: https://github.com/Axurios/Erdos/blob/main/erdos684_new.pdf To finish, and have the $C\ln(n)\ln\ln(n)$ upper-bound, I am just missing the $\quad \underset{??}{=}\quad $ equality:\[ \omega_k(\binom{n}{k}) := \underset{\sqrt{k}\leq p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \mathbb{1}_{\left( \{\frac{n}{p}\} > \{\frac{k}{p}\}\right)} \quad \underset{??}{=} \underset{\sqrt{k}\leq p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \left\{\frac{k}{p}\right\} \,\,+ \,\, o(\pi(k)) \quad = (1-\gamma)\,\pi(k) \,+\, o(\pi(k)) \]for $k\sim C \ln(n) \ln (\ln( n))$ : This seems doable, as if $0 \leq \textstyle\left\{\frac{n}{p}\right\} < 1 $ is uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$, $\mathbb{P}\Big(\left\{\frac{n}{p}\right\} < \left\{\frac{k}{p}\right\} \Big) = \left\{\frac{k}{p}\right\}$ and would also worked if proved for $2\leq p \leq k$ PS: edited for brevity

Thanks, that does sound more reasonable, but this is similar to the heuristic that natso26 has provided in another comment (in fact a little weaker, since it only addresses $\omega_k$ rather than the full $p$-adic valuation. As such this is (a) likely only an 'almost all $n$' heuristic, (b) will likely be very difficult to prove, and (c) only gives $k\ll \log n\log\log n$ rather than the stronger $k\sim 2\log n$ that natso26's heuristic predicts. Also, please do not include such long AI-generated proofs as a comment, these take up a lot of space and are difficult to read (and often include a lot of unnecessary bloat compared to a human-written proof). If you have some interesting AI-generated ideas you'd like to share, it suffices to link to the AI transcript or some other externally hosted account. Future lengthy comments of this style will be rejected.

Thanks for the reply, I will only link the pdf and the main idea for my future comments (I wasn't sure what the appropriate length was), but for the record, my two previous comments were not AI generated, and typed by hand in overleaf, I've used AI just to search references, see if existing results did what I wanted, and verify that what I was saying made sense. Sorry if my writing style is poor/hard to read, I'm still a student. I felt that what I found, and how well it matched with my python experiments could be interesting enough to be shared, as I started to work on this before any comments were made by others, concerning Natso post, I'm not sure that I understand, why we could not prove the result : \( \underset{\sqrt{k}\leq p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \mathbb{1}_{\left( \{\frac{n}{p}\} > \{\frac{k}{p}\}\right)} \quad \underset{??}{=} \,\,\underset{\sqrt{k}\leq p\leq k, \,p\in \mathcal{P}}{\sum} \left\{\frac{k}{p}\right\} \,\,+ \,\, o(\pi(k)) \quad \) (for $k\sim C \ln(n) \ln (\ln( n))$) without using the uniform distribution heuristic, and the difference it would make. But might be a "me" problem :) PS: do you want me to edit my previous comment to be shorter (lefting the pdf file and a really short summary of the final state of my "reflexion") ?

Ah, apologies, I'm so used to seeing GPT links in these comments now that I thought your github link was to a GPT transcript. No worries about your writing style, this is something that we all develop with experience. In general 'appropriate length' here is context dependent, but generally speaking it's 'the minimum that is needed to get the important ideas across to another mathematician', so you can leave spelling out all the gory details to an externally linked PDF. It's of course hard to answer any question like 'why can't we prove...', perhaps a proof is possible, it just feels difficult to me to turn that heuristic into a proof without substantial new ideas. EDIT: Yes, if you could truncate your previous comment that would be appreciated.

I asked Gemini-3 for its opinion. It says https://gemini.google.com/share/59efa34162de For me it was fun to read, in particular the list with the weak points. In particular the complaint that you used "circular validation" by asking GPT again and again. On the other hand: the weaknesses found be Gemini are not really heavy. The comments read (for me) a bit like those of a sore loser.

I want to say that “strengths and weaknesses” is not the usual way of thinking when reviewing math papers. This seems to confuse Gemini a bit and make it list things like presentation strengths/weaknesses, or strategy strengths/weaknesses, as well. If the goal is correctness + significance + exposition, as per usual reviews, may be: “Can you review this paper?”, “Can you check this paper for correctness?”, “Read the paper carefully and look for possible errors.”, etc.
